0:
**Part 1 – Personal Biography (169 words)**  

I’m a 51‑year‑old man living in Zaltbommel, a town I’ve called home for the past twenty‑four years. After completing an HBO program in Logistics Management, I started as a warehouse supervisor for a regional distribution firm and eventually became the operations manager. My workday usually begins at 7 a.m. with a quick coffee at the local bakery, followed by a commute on my electric bike to the depot on the outskirts of town.  

At home I share a house with my wife, Marijke, and our two children—Lena, 17, who plays volleyball, and Joris, 14, who is into coding. On weekends we often visit my parents in nearby Gelderland, helping them with grocery trips and small repairs. I’m an avid member of the town’s historical society, spending evenings cataloguing old photographs and writing short pieces for the local newsletter. When I can, I tend to my modest vegetable garden, which supplies most of our salad greens during summer. I value reliability, community, and the quiet rhythm of everyday life.  

---

**Part 2 – Political Perspective (152 words)**  

Having spent three decades overseeing supply chains, I’ve seen how predictable tax policies and stable business regulations keep a company’s cash flow steady. When the municipality upgraded the road network near the depot, delivery times dropped noticeably, and the cost savings filtered down to the workers’ wages. Those practical improvements matter to me more than abstract promises.  

My parents rely on a clear, affordable pension system, and I’ve watched the government’s incremental adjustments keep their monthly income sufficient for modest travel and occasional medical needs. The local schools’ focus on applied skills—something I support as a parent—benefits my children’s readiness for the job market without demanding radical curriculum overhauls.  

Because I prioritize economic continuity, efficient public services, and incremental reforms that protect families like mine, I feel the VVD’s approach aligns best with the day‑to‑day realities I experience.
12:
**Part 1 – Personal Biography**  

I’m a thirty‑year‑old who grew up in a suburb of Arnhem, the kind of place where the canals wind past old brick warehouses and the weekend market buzzes with fresh produce. After finishing secondary school, I spent a couple of years in a vocational program before moving into a hands‑on apprenticeship in electronics, eventually transitioning to a junior developer role at a mid‑size tech firm. My days usually start with a quick run along the nearby river, followed by a coffee at the same corner café where I catch up on the latest tech podcasts.  

Outside of work I’m an avid cyclist; I’ve logged countless rides through the Veluwe, often stopping at local breweries to sample seasonal brews. I share a flat with two friends—one studying architecture and the other training to become a teacher—and we host movie nights that usually end with heated debates about sci‑fi plot twists. On Sundays I volunteer at a community garden, tending to tomatoes and kale, and I try to keep my bookshelf stocked with a mix of programming manuals, Dutch literature, and a few mystery novels.  

**Part 2 – Political Perspective**  

My everyday experience navigating a rapidly evolving tech sector has taught me how crucial adaptable education and continuous learning are. Watching colleagues struggle with outdated curricula convinced me that a flexible, forward‑thinking approach to schooling benefits both individuals and the economy. I’ve also seen how digital infrastructure—or the lack of it—directly affects small businesses like the café I frequent; reliable broadband can mean the difference between thriving and closing its doors.  

Living in a city that balances historic charm with modern growth, I’ve felt the impact of climate‑friendly policies firsthand—cleaner riverbanks, expanded bike lanes, and incentives for renewable energy projects make my commute smoother and the air fresher. These tangible improvements shape my belief that pragmatic, evidence‑based policies can enhance quality of life without sacrificing economic vitality. When I head to the polls, I look for parties that prioritize education reform, digital innovation, and sustainable urban planning, trusting that these choices will keep my community resilient and forward‑moving.
182:
**Part 1 – Personal Biography**  

I’m a 22‑year‑old living in the heart of Amsterdam, where I share a modest two‑bedroom flat with a close friend I met during my first year of university. My days usually start with a quick jog along the canals, followed by a bike ride to the coworking space where I freelance as a graphic designer for indie game studios. Mornings are a blend of client calls and sketching concepts, while afternoons often find me in the city library, researching modern typography or catching up on a novel by Haruki Murakami.  

When the weekend rolls around, I love exploring the flea markets in De Pijp, hunting for vintage vinyl records, and then cooking a simple dinner—usually a Dutch pea soup with fresh rye bread—while a playlist of lo‑fi beats plays in the background. I’m a regular at a local community garden, where I help tend to herbs and tomatoes; the work grounds me and reminds me of the importance of green spaces in an urban setting.  

I’m also part of a small group of friends who meet monthly to discuss contemporary art exhibitions and debate the latest tech gadgets. These gatherings keep me curious and constantly learning beyond my professional sphere.  

---

**Part 2 – Political Perspective**  

My voting choice reflects the ways everyday life in a dense, multicultural city shapes my priorities. Navigating crowded trams and paying steep rents has shown me how essential affordable, well‑maintained public transport and housing policies are for young people trying to stay independent. Watching the city’s climate‑action initiatives—from the expansion of bike lanes to the push for renewable energy in municipal buildings—has convinced me that progressive, evidence‑based solutions work when backed by clear planning.  

Having experienced the challenges of balancing freelance work with continuous learning, I value policies that make higher education and vocational training more accessible and adaptable to rapid technological change. The prospect of a transparent digital infrastructure, where personal data is protected yet innovation is encouraged, feels vital for my field and for the broader creative community.  

Overall, I’m looking for a government that invests in pragmatic, forward‑thinking measures—whether it’s smarter urban planning, climate resilience, or education reform—because those are the changes that directly improve the rhythm of my daily life.
19:
**Part 1 – Personal Biography**  

I’m twenty and live on the island of Goeree‑Overflakkee with my parents and my little brother, who’s still in primary school. My dad runs a modest dairy farm, and on weekends I often help out milking the cows or loading the milk trucks. In the mornings I’m at the local supermarket, stacking shelves and helping customers while studying for my mbo‑1 program in hospitality.  

When I’m not working or in class, I love cycling the flat, wind‑blown roads that circle the island, especially the trail that leads to the old lighthouse. I’m a member of the women’s volleyball team at the community sports hall, and we meet twice a week for practice and occasional matches against nearby towns. I also volunteer at the library once a month, shelving books and running a small reading club for kids who love detective stories.  

Music is a big part of my life—I’m always humming along to Dutch pop from the early 2000s while I’m cooking dinner with my mom. I value reliability, a good laugh with friends, and the quiet sense of belonging that comes from knowing most people in my town.  

---

**Part 2 – Political Perspective**  

Living on a small island has shown me how national policies often feel distant from the everyday realities I face. My part‑time job at the supermarket means I’m sensitive to the cost of living; when fuel prices rise, the price of the groceries I stock goes up, and my family’s farm feels the squeeze too. Public transport here is sparse, so I spend most of my time cycling or driving, which makes me question the emphasis politicians place on urban‑centric solutions.  

I’ve watched local council meetings where concrete issues—like improving the road to the dairy or expanding broadband for small businesses—were discussed, while national debates seemed to swirl around topics that rarely touch my life. Because I rarely see my concerns reflected in party platforms, I’ve become hesitant to cast a ballot that feels symbolic rather than impactful. Instead, I focus my energy on community projects, like the library reading club and the volleyball team, where I can see tangible results. I remain open to change but prefer to stay grounded in the practical changes that affect my neighborhood directly.
23:
**Part 1 – Personal Biography**

I’m a 25‑year‑old guy living in a modest apartment on the outskirts of Veenendaal, just a short bike ride from the train station. After finishing a mix of Vmbo, a year of havo‑/vwo‑onderbouw, and completing an MBO‑1 program in logistics, I landed a full‑time job as a warehouse operative for a regional distribution centre. My shift usually starts at 6 a.m., so I’m often the first one there, loading pallets and coordinating incoming shipments.  

When I’m not at work, I spend my evenings tinkering with my old motorcycle—doing the occasional maintenance myself—and I’m part of a small amateur football team that meets twice a week at the local sports field. I enjoy listening to Dutch indie rock while cooking simple meals; my favorite dish is a homemade stir‑fry with fresh veggies from the Saturday market.  

I’m close to my younger sister, who studies nursing, and we often help our parents with household chores on weekends. I value reliability, practical problem‑solving, and the quiet satisfaction of getting things done, whether it’s fixing a leak or meeting a delivery deadline.

**Part 2 – Political Perspective**

Because most of my day is structured around early starts, tight schedules, and hands‑on work, I often feel that the political conversation circles around issues that don’t touch my daily reality. I’ve seen promises about “modernising logistics” turn into vague plans, while the real bottlenecks—like unpredictable shift patterns and the rising cost of commuting—remain unaddressed.  

When I look at the parties, I struggle to find a clear line between what they say and what actually improves the conditions for people like me on the warehouse floor. I’ve tried to follow debates, but the rapid turnover of topics and the focus on high‑level policy feel distant from the concrete challenges I face, such as scheduling flexibility and fair wages.  

That disconnect leads me to sit out the vote. I’m not opposed to politics; I just don’t see a candidate that convincingly bridges the gap between rhetoric and the practical needs of workers in my sector. For now, I prefer to keep my energy on improving my own situation and supporting my community in more immediate ways.
